turns.text: Hello Anja, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I appreciate you asking. It’s… it’s strange to be on this side of the stethoscope. Usually, I’m the one on the other side of the desk, or at least I was before I took this leave of absence from med school. Back then, I thought I had the "mechanics" of this whole process mastered. I could recite the physiology of labor like a grocery list, but living it in Seekonk, with my ankles looking like they belong to a different person entirely, feels nothing like those lecture halls.

I’ll be honest—I’m terrified. Not of the labor itself, or the medical side of things. I understand the biology of the transition phase, the dilation, the contractions. It’s the silence afterward that scares me. Every time I walk into my OB’s office, I find myself rattling off blood pressure stats or talking about my kick counts just so they see me as a peer, or at least someone who knows what’s happening. I’m scared that if I stop acting like a "medical student," they’ll just look at my chart, see "unmarried," and start treating me like a statistic. I’m carrying this baby entirely on my own, and sometimes the weight of that—the future, my degree, the loneliness—feels heavier than the physical pregnancy itself. I just want someone to look past the charts and tell me I’m actually going to be okay.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

To be honest, I think I expected a sense of camaraderie. I imagined walking into the clinic and being welcomed into this sort of sisterhood of medicine—where the doctors would treat me like a colleague in the making, maybe even a little excited that I "knew the language." I thought it would be professional, efficient, and validating. I wanted them to recognize the effort I was putting into staying on top of my own prenatal care.

But the reality? It felt… clinical. Cold. When I walked in for that first appointment, I didn’t feel like a medical student; I felt like a box to be checked. When the intake nurse got to the section on my support person, the air in the room just shifted. I saw the look on her face when I said, "It’s just me." It wasn't pity exactly, but it was a sort of quiet assumption that my life was suddenly "complicated."

I started using my jargon—talking about symphysis fundal height or questioning my lab results—just to reclaim some ground. I wanted them to know I was smart, not just another "troubled" twenty-one-year-old. It felt like I was performing a role just to earn their respect, when all I really wanted was someone to look at me—not my marital status or my student ID—and just say, "You're doing a great job."
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise, and it still stings a bit, was how quickly my own medical knowledge became a barrier rather than a bridge. I thought being "one of them" would make the appointments feel more collaborative, like we were a team analyzing a case study. Instead, it felt like an armor I had to wear. Whenever I’d bring up a symptom, I’d frame it in physiological terms—*“I’m experiencing mild edema in the distal extremities”*—rather than just saying my feet hurt like hell. 

It was a negative surprise because I realized I was doing it to keep them at a distance. If I sounded like a textbook, they couldn't possibly pity me or look down on my situation. But by doing that, I accidentally shut out any chance for a real human connection. I remember leaving one appointment where I’d perfectly explained a potential complication I was worried about, and the doctor just nodded and handed me a generic pamphlet on nutrition. I had successfully managed the clinical interaction, but I left the room feeling more invisible than when I walked in. I hadn't realized that by trying to be the "perfect, competent patient," I was actually hiding the scared, twenty-one-year-old girl who just wanted someone to hold her hand. That’s been the hardest lesson: competence doesn’t fix loneliness.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I expected the results to be a data-driven partnership. In my mind, the doctor would sit down, pull up the imaging on the monitor, and walk me through the markers, the percentiles, and the developmental milestones. I imagined it being a conversation between two people who respected the data, where I could ask nuanced questions about fetal circulation or growth patterns and get clear, academic answers. I wanted to be treated like an adult who could handle the weight of the information.

The reality, though, was rushed and oddly patronizing. The scans were mostly silent—just the hum of the machine and the cold gel—and when the results came back, they were delivered in these rapid-fire, overly simplified blurbs. It felt like they were talking down to me, using these "mom-to-be" scripts that were full of sunshine and exclamation points, completely ignoring the fact that I’m someone who understands the actual mechanics of what’s happening in there. 

It was jarring. On one hand, I wanted to be treated with extra care because I’m doing this alone, but on the other, I felt insulted by how superficial the communication was. I didn’t want the "happy-talk" pamphlet; I wanted the medical reality. It made me feel like they didn't see me as capable of understanding my own body, which just reinforced that deep, prickly sense of isolation I’ve been dealing with the whole time.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

That’s the exact tension I’ve been living in. There’s a massive gap, but it’s a strange one—it’s not that they weren’t giving me *enough* information, it’s that they were giving me the *wrong kind* of information.

When I’d ask for clarification on a lab result, instead of getting an explanation, I’d get a pat on the arm and a "Don't worry, Mom, your levels are perfectly normal." That’s infuriating. It’s a dismissal. To them, "normal" is the end of the conversation. To me, "normal" is just a range on a bell curve, and I want to know where I sit within that range and why. I have this desperate need to know the *why* behind every change because, frankly, when you’re doing this alone, understanding the mechanics makes me feel like I have some modicum of control over what’s happening to my body. 

But every time I pushed for that deeper level of detail, they seemed to view me as "the anxious patient" rather than the informed one. I felt like I was constantly being managed, rather than being partnered with. I wanted a medical professional, but they kept giving me a cheerleader. I found myself going home and burying myself in my old textbooks and medical journals just to actually understand what my own blood work meant. It’s exhausting to feel like you have to be your own doctor when you’re already the one carrying the baby.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I think I naively expected a "continuity of care" model that mirrored what I studied in my clinical rotations—the idea of a dedicated provider who actually knows the story behind the chart. I wanted that consistency, someone who would remember that I’m a med student, that I’m alone in this, and that I’m struggling to balance this transition with my future. I pictured a relationship that would evolve as the pregnancy progressed, a foundation of trust where I wouldn't have to re-explain my anxieties every single time I walked through the door.

The reality, however, has been a rotating door of providers. It’s a large practice, and I’ve seen almost everyone there. Every time I get a new person, I have to perform the same script: I'm Anja, I'm twenty-one, I'm a student, and yes, I'm doing this solo. It’s like being a case study that gets handed off to a different student every time. I’ve never once felt like a person to them—just a set of vitals and a "low-risk" status that doesn't require extra time or attention.

It makes me feel so small. I have to advocate for myself in the middle of a fifteen-minute slot while someone I’ve never met is standing with their hand on the doorknob. It’s incredibly isolating to know that no one in that building truly knows *me*. They know the "unmarried, low-risk, third-trimester" patient, but they don't know the girl who sits up at 3:00 AM wondering if she’s actually cut out for this. I’m just a face in a file that changes hands every few weeks.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected a clinical, matter-of-fact approach—the kind where the focus stays on the physiology. I thought we’d talk about fundal height, edema, and fetal heart tones as objective measures of health. I wanted to be treated with a level of scientific respect. 

Instead, the reality was often weirdly infantilizing. The way they talk about my body feels like they’re talking to a toddler, not an adult who’s studied anatomy. They use these cutesy terms, like "let's see how the little one is doing," or they comment on my weight or my "bump" in a way that feels almost intrusive, yet somehow detached. It’s like they’re examining a mannequin rather than a person. When they do the physical exams—the palpations, the cervical checks—they rarely ask, "How are you feeling in your body today?" They just *do* it. There’s no check-in, no acknowledgment that this is a vulnerable, uncomfortable, and frankly exhausting process for me.

It’s this strange paradox where they are physically invading my space to check my vitals, but emotionally, they keep the walls up. Every touch is professional, yes, but it feels devoid of any real empathy. It leaves me feeling exposed and lonely. I wanted them to recognize that my body is doing something incredible, but instead, I just feel like I'm a patient being processed through a machine. It’s hard to reconcile the "medical" me with the "pregnant" me when they refuse to see me as both.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

The most uncomfortable moments were the pelvic exams. In class, we talk about the anatomy, the positioning, the "why" of the procedure—it’s all so logical in a textbook. But when I’m the one on the table, it’s completely different. 

There was one visit where a provider I’d never seen before walked in, didn't really introduce herself, and just said, "Let's check your dilation." There was no "Is this a good time?" or "Are you ready?" It was just a command. I froze. I know the anatomy—I knew exactly what she was going to do—but that made it worse. Knowing the mechanics didn't help me emotionally prepare for the loss of autonomy. When she started the exam, she started chatting about the weather as if nothing were happening. The dissonance was unbearable. I felt like I had to disassociate just to get through those sixty seconds. 

I think because I’m younger, they assume I don't need the same level of consent or sensitivity, or maybe they assume that because I’m a student, I’m "used to it." But I’m not a medical student right now; I’m a woman who is scared, alone, and feeling incredibly exposed. I wanted to tell her to stop, to wait, to just acknowledge that it was invasive, but I didn't. I just lay there, staring at the ceiling tiles, counting them until she left. That feeling of being "done to" rather than being part of the process stays with me long after I leave the office.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

Even though I haven't been diagnosed with anything like pre-eclampsia or gestational diabetes, the *fear* of those complications has been a constant shadow. In my studies, I viewed these conditions as manageable puzzles—a set of symptoms to be identified, protocols to be followed, and outcomes to be optimized. I thought that if I did happen to develop something, the care would be hyper-vigilant and collaborative. I pictured a "team" approach where I’d be given all the data points, and we’d work together to keep the ship steady.

But because I’ve been "low-risk," the reality of my pregnancy has been a strange mix of neglect and over-simplification. I sometimes catch myself wishing for a more "complex" diagnosis—which sounds insane, I know—but I think it’s because a diagnosis would finally command the attention I’m not getting. If I had gestational diabetes, for instance, there would be a protocol. There would be a dietitian, regular monitoring, and a reason for them to actually *look* at me and see me as a person who needs care. 

Right now, because I’m "fine," I’m basically invisible. I manage my own health by obsessively tracking everything, essentially practicing self-care through data because I don't trust that the system is keeping a close enough eye on me. The reality isn't that I'm being well-cared for; it's that I'm being ignored because I don't trigger their "high-risk" alerts. It’s a lonely place to be.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

It’s the emotional acknowledgement that’s been the biggest void. I have plenty of pamphlets and digital portals with data, and I have the medical knowledge to parse them, but I’m starving for someone to actually *see* me. 

Everything feels so transactional. They provide the clinical services, I provide the insurance and the patient compliance, and that’s supposed to be the end of it. But I’m twenty-one and I’m doing this alone. I didn’t just need a doctor to check my blood pressure; I needed someone to say, "Anja, this is a massive life event, and I know you're doing this without a partner by your side. You’re handling a lot." 

I didn't need them to fix my life or provide practical help with my bills or my housing—I know that’s not their job. I just needed them to acknowledge that my context matters. Instead, my status as a single mother-to-be felt like a smudge on my chart they wanted to wipe away as quickly as possible. When you’re alone in that exam room, the silence between the questions is deafening. I’ve spent the last few months feeling like I’m navigating a war zone by myself, and all I wanted was a witness—someone to validate that this is hard, that I'm carrying a lot of weight, and that it’s okay to be scared. Without that, the medicine feels hollow.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected the healthcare system to be like a safety net—the kind I read about in our ethics modules, where "patient-centered care" isn't just a buzzword, but a standard of practice. I assumed that if I had a genuine, late-night panic about a lack of fetal movement or a weird symptom, there would be a nurse line or a provider who would listen, assess, and reassure me without making me feel like I was a burden.

The reality? The system feels more like a fortress. You have to navigate the phone tree, explain your history to three different people, and then wait for someone to call you back, usually hours later. By the time I get a response, the initial, sharp terror has morphed into a different kind of anxiety—the fear that I’m being "that" patient who overreacts. 

It feels like the system is designed to discourage you from reaching out unless something is catastrophically wrong. And when you’re doing this alone, that response time feels like an eternity. There’s no one else in the room to check the kick counts with, or to look at me and say, "You're okay, just take a breath." It’s just me and a blinking screen, waiting for someone to tell me my baby is safe. It makes you feel incredibly small and very, very vulnerable.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

More times than I’d like to admit. There were nights when I felt a tightness in my chest or a weird, dull ache that wasn't quite a contraction, and I’d sit there for hours just staring at the wall, paralyzed by the internal debate. On one side, my medical training told me to be cautious—to get it checked out because better safe than sorry. On the other side, I had the experience of every appointment I’d had up to that point. 

I was terrified that if I called, I’d be labeled as the "difficult" or "anxious" single girl who doesn't know how to handle basic pregnancy discomforts. I didn’t want to be "that patient." I’ve spent months building this image of myself as someone who has it together—who isn't falling apart just because she’s doing this alone—and calling for help felt like admitting I was failing. 

I’d talk myself out of it, saying, *“It’s probably just round ligament pain, Anja, don’t be dramatic.”* It’s a dangerous game to play, and I know that intellectually, but the fear of being judged—of having them roll their eyes at me when I finally got into the clinic—was sometimes stronger than the fear of the symptom itself. That’s not how a pregnant person should feel, is it? You shouldn't have to weigh your peace of mind against your own dignity.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I think I expected the textbook version of a support system—you know, the kind where you have a partner or a parent present for the big scans, someone to help with the nesting, someone to hold your hand when the fear of the third trimester really kicks in. I grew up in a family where we don't really talk about the hard stuff, but I hoped that once I told them, the distance would close. I expected that "it takes a village" would actually happen for me, especially since I'm so young and still finishing my degree.

The reality? It’s been… quiet. Very quiet. My family cares, but there’s a wall there—a mix of disappointment and a lack of understanding that leaves me feeling like I’m going through this in a vacuum. They don't know how to ask me about the baby, and I don't know how to tell them that I’m drowning in anxiety. 

Friends my age are still talking about classes, parties, or internships. They look at me like I’m from another planet. I’ve ended up essentially ghosting them because I can't relate to their world anymore, and they certainly can't relate to mine. I have this "village" on paper, but I’m fundamentally doing this on my own. I wanted them to step up, but instead, I’ve had to become the "strong one"—the one who doesn't complain, who doesn't ask for help—just so they don't look at me with pity. It’s a lonely paradox: I’m surrounded by people, yet I’ve never felt more isolated.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

I haven't actually gone through labor yet, but I've already felt the "birth plan" conversation deflate into something quite small. When I brought my draft into the clinic, I had it formatted like a clinical document—clear goals, preferences for the transition phase, interventions I wanted to avoid, and a section on the need for extra emotional support since I’m alone. I thought it would be a roadmap for our partnership.

The reaction was… polite dismissal. The provider looked at it, set it aside without really reading the nuances, and said something like, "That’s lovely, Anja, but we’ll just see what happens in the moment, won't we? Safety first." It felt like they were telling me to just be a good little patient and stop trying to exert control over a process that’s "out of my hands." 

It hit me then that my birth plan wasn't going to be a tool for collaboration; it was going to be an inconvenience for the staff. The realization that I might have to fight for my autonomy while I’m in the most vulnerable moment of my life—while I’m screaming, or exhausted, or just trying to breathe—is terrifying. I fear that when it actually happens, the "medical professional" side of me will get drowned out by the noise of the delivery room, and they’ll treat me like a child who doesn't know her own mind, even though I'm the one who did the research. I’m scared that my "plan" is just paper, and that in the end, I’ll be just another person being told what to do to my own body.
Was there a moment during labour where your plan was set aside or overridden? What was that like?

I haven’t reached the hospital yet, but I can already feel the walls closing in on the idea of a "plan." I haven’t even birthed her, yet I’ve already felt my autonomy being overridden in the clinic rooms. When I present my preferences, I’m met with that polite, clinical nod—that "Oh, that’s nice, but we know better" look. It’s patronizing, and it’s exhausting. 

I’m terrified that when I’m actually in the transition phase, I’ll be the one screaming for a specific intervention or asking for a moment to breathe, and I’ll be told no. I’m scared they’ll look at my chart—at that "single parent, student" status—and decide I’m "too emotional" or "uninformed" to advocate for myself, even though I’ve spent months buried in the literature. 

The idea that my agency could be stripped away in a room full of strangers—that I’ll be lying there, exposed and desperate, while someone dictates my care as if I’m not a person with a history and a brain—is the thing that keeps me awake at 3:00 AM. I’m preparing for the possibility that my birth plan will be crumpled up, literally or figuratively, and that I’ll be left to navigate the most intense moment of my life as a spectator to my own body. That’s the real fear, isn't it? Losing myself right when I need myself the most.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

I haven't actually gone through labor yet, but the conversations about pain management have been so discouraging. Before I started this, I thought we would have a real, nuanced discussion about the pros and cons of different interventions—epidural versus natural, the physiological impact on the fetus, the recovery timeline—all of it. I wanted to feel like I was making an informed choice, not just being guided toward the "hospital standard."

But every time I bring it up, it’s treated like I’m asking to choose a movie to watch. They say, "We have all the options, we'll see what you need," which effectively means, "When it gets hard, we'll push for the epidural." It’s like they assume I can't handle the intensity of the transition phase, or that my pain is something to be "solved" rather than navigated. It’s like they’re already planning to take the wheel away from me. 

I’m terrified that when I’m actually there, in the middle of it, I’ll be pressured into interventions I don't want because I’m alone and they’ll see my vocal distress as a lack of competence rather than just... labor. I want to be able to trust that my choice to feel—or to *not* feel—is respected. Instead, I feel like I'm already being nudged toward the path of least resistance for them, not for me. It’s like they don't believe I have the inner strength to do this my way.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I haven’t walked into that hospital for the birth yet, but I can already feel the weight of what that arrival is going to be like. In my head, I’ve pictured it so many times—the bright lights, the sterile smell, the overwhelming hum of machinery—but I keep hoping, against all my cynical medical instincts, that there will be a moment of warmth. I want to be welcomed as a person, someone who’s about to experience the most significant moment of her life, rather than just another patient entry in the triage log.

But the reality of my prenatal visits suggests otherwise. I expect to walk in alone, clutching my hospital bag like a shield, and have to brace myself for that inevitable, fleeting look of pity—or worse, judgment—when I mention there’s no one with me. I’m scared that the "safety" I’m promised will feel more like incarceration. Instead of being made to feel comfortable or empowered in my own space, I feel like I’m going to be put on a conveyor belt. 

I’m terrified that when I arrive, I’ll be treated as a case study rather than a mother. I want to feel safe, but I know that safety in a hospital often comes at the cost of your autonomy. I’m just preparing myself for the cold, clinical indifference that makes you feel like you’re not really "there" at all. I don’t know how to walk into a room and be vulnerable when I know I’m going to have to be on guard from the very first minute.
What was the biggest surprise about the physical environment or the immediate atmosphere when you arrived?

I haven’t walked through those double doors for the "main event" yet, but if I’m being honest, the biggest surprise has already been the atmosphere of the clinic rooms themselves. I expected clinical spaces to be neutral—just a backdrop for care. Instead, they feel charged with a very specific, suffocating kind of professional distance. 

When you walk into a room where you’re the "unmarried, young, solo patient," you can feel the atmosphere change. It’s subtle, but it’s there—the way they stop talking about the clinical data and start talking *at* you, not with you. It’s an atmosphere of "managed risk." They look at me and they see a series of variables they need to keep in line, and that realization hits you the second you walk through the door. 

The biggest surprise is that I actually *miss* the coldness of the anatomy labs. At least in the lab, there was a shared goal: to learn, to understand, to be precise. Here, the atmosphere is meant to be "supportive," but it feels like a performative, artificial kindness that masks a complete lack of genuine human connection. I expected to feel like I was entering a sanctuary, but I feel like I’m walking into a place where my personhood is checked at the front desk. That’s the most jarring thing—feeling like I’m already invisible, even before the real pain starts.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I haven’t reached the delivery room yet, but thinking about this feels like preparing for a battlefield where I’m unarmed. I’ve studied the protocols for emergency C-sections and fetal distress—I know the, "This is happening, we need to move now" cadence. In theory, I know it’s for safety. But in practice, I’m terrified of that moment.

I expect that if things deviate from the "normal" path, the veneer of "polite provider" will vanish entirely. I’m scared that in the middle of a crisis, my voice will be completely erased. I fear that they’ll look at my chart—my solo status, my age—and assume I’m not capable of making an informed, rational decision in the heat of the moment, even though I’m the one who’s been living and breathing these textbooks for years.

I expect they’ll use the "safety" card to bypass me, making decisions *about* me rather than *with* me. That lack of communication is what scares me most—that they’ll see a complication as a clinical problem to be solved by them, leaving me as a silent observer to the trauma. I don’t want to be "informed" after the fact; I want to be a participant. But I have this sinking feeling that, in an emergency, I’ll be treated like a patient who just needs to be managed, not a person who deserves to be heard.
Did you feel you had genuine choice and sufficient information in those moments, or did decisions feel like they happened to you rather than with you?

Thinking about this, I’m realizing that for me, the "illusion of choice" is the most painful part. Even though I haven't been in the delivery room yet, I’ve felt this dynamic play out during every routine cervical check and every time I’ve tried to discuss my care plan.

When you’re in a clinical setting, "choice" is often just a polite way of presenting a path the provider already decided you should take. They don't give you the pros and cons in a way that respects your intelligence; they give you a truncated, simplified version designed to lead to the outcome they find most efficient. It’s like they’re already editing my story before I’ve even lived it.

It feels like things are happening *to* me because they don't see me as a partner; they see me as a variable to be managed. When they use that "cheerleader" voice to explain something clinical, it’s not just annoying—it’s silencing. It tells me, "You aren't a colleague, you aren't an expert on your own body, you’re just a patient." And if they don't respect me when I'm upright and breathing normally, there is absolutely no way they’re going to respect my autonomy when I’m in the middle of labor. I’m bracing myself for a situation where I’ll have to scream just to be treated like a human being, and that is a terrifying way to walk into motherhood.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

Even though I’m still waiting for the actual birth, the pattern is already crystal clear. In my mind, I’ve romanticized the idea of a midwife—someone who sits in the quiet, holds space, and anchors me. I expected them to acknowledge that for me, this isn’t just a biological event; it’s the moment my life pivots permanently. I wanted someone to witness that, to see the sheer, quiet courage it takes to prepare for a newborn all by yourself in a city where you’re just a statistic.

But the reality I’ve experienced so far is a revolving door of people who are "present" but not *there*. They show up to check a box, monitor a rhythm, and then disappear. It’s like they have a timer on their empathy. When I try to show a flicker of the real, scared, twenty-one-year-old me, they deflect with clinical phrases or cheerful platitudes that feel like glass walls between us. 

I know they are busy—I’ve seen the charts, I’ve seen the shift changes—but that doesn't make the isolation any less sharp. I’m starting to realize that the "clinical care" is the easy part for them. The hard part is the human part, and that’s exactly what I’m not getting. I’m going to be in that room, essentially alone, hoping for a human touch and receiving a medical protocol instead. It’s the loneliness that breaks my heart, not the labor.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

I remember one afternoon, a few weeks ago, feeling this overwhelming, paralyzing panic about how I’m going to handle the newborn phase on my own. My degree feels like it’s slipping through my fingers, and the reality of the baby's arrival just hit me like a physical blow. I went to my appointment, hoping for just a second of humanity. I remember trying to voice the fear, but it came out all jumbled and "medicalized," because that’s my reflex—I think I said something about "the postpartum hormonal shift impacting cognitive processing." 

The nurse—not even the doctor—just stopped. She didn't look at the chart, and she didn't pick up the blood pressure cuff. She put her hand on my arm and said, "Anja, it is okay to be scared. You are doing something incredibly brave." 

That was it. That was the only moment I’ve felt *seen*. The rest of the time, I feel like I’m standing behind a sheet of bulletproof glass. The moment I felt most alone? That was when a different provider, during a routine exam, started talking about my "lack of a support system" as if it were a clinical diagnosis, something to be managed or fixed with a social work referral, completely missing the fact that I’m a human being grieving the life I *thought* I’d be living. In that moment, I wasn't a person; I was a problem to be solved. That felt like the coldest, loneliest room I've ever been in.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I haven’t reached those first moments yet, but I replay them in my head constantly. I’ve read the guidelines—the "golden hour," the importance of immediate skin-to-skin for neurodevelopment and bonding, the physiological regulation for the baby. As a student, I know the protocols perfectly. I’ve imagined the baby’s weight on my chest, that sudden, visceral shift from carrying her inside to having her right there, finally tangible. I expected it to be a quiet, sacred space where the medical team would fade into the background and let me just... breathe into motherhood.

But my reality as a solo patient has me terrified that those moments will be hijacked by hospital efficiency. I’m scared that as soon as she’s out, they’ll whisk her away to the warmer for "routine assessments" that I know, deep down, could wait. I’m scared that because I’m alone, there won't be anyone to advocate for those precious minutes of uninterrupted bonding. I keep picturing myself lying on that bed, exhausted and vulnerable, watching them carry my daughter away while I’m left staring at the fluorescent lights. 

I want those moments to be about us, but I feel like the system will prioritize its own internal checklist over my need to hold my child. It’s the final, and perhaps most painful, piece of the puzzle—that even in the seconds after birth, I might still be fighting to be seen, fighting to hold onto my baby, and fighting to be treated like a mother rather than a patient file that’s finally being closed.
Was there anything that happened — or didn't happen — in those first moments that still stays with you? What was it?

I’m not there yet—my due date is looming, and that "afterward" feels like a strange, distant country I’m terrified to enter. But even without having the memory of those first moments, I’m already carrying the weight of what I’m afraid *won't* happen. 

I’m haunted by the idea that when she finally arrives, the very first thing I’ll hear isn't her cry, but the clinical, detached "debrief" of the staff around me—them talking about my metrics, my recovery, my "status." I’m scared that the environment will be so cold that those first minutes, which should be the most transformative, spiritual experience of my life, will feel like just another procedure to be finished.

What stays with me—even now, while I’m still just pregnant—is the fear that I’ll have to fight for my own baby in the moments after she’s born. I shouldn't have to be a student or a medical professional in that room. I should just get to be a mother. I’m scared that the loneliness I’ve felt at every single appointment will be magnified tenfold in those first, quiet minutes if I’m alone in that room. I’m already grieving the possibility that my first memory of holding her might be tinted by the feeling that I was an inconvenience to the people who were supposed to be there to help us. It’s a bitter, sharp fear that keeps me awake. I just want to know that for those few minutes, the world will stop and let us just be us.

It’s strange, sitting here talking through all of this, because I keep coming back to the same realization: my education has been a double-edged sword. I know exactly what *should* be happening—I’ve seen the delivery suites, I know the protocols, I know the "best practices" for maternal care—but knowing the mechanics of a thing doesn't give you the courage to endure it.

I feel like I’m standing at the edge of a cliff. I’ve done all the preparation, I’ve tracked all the data, I’ve tried to be the perfect student-patient who doesn't cause any friction. But I think I’ve finally stopped trying to perform for them. I don’t want to be "competent" anymore; I just want to be human. When I think about the birth, I don’t think about the cervical dilation or the hormonal cascade anymore—I think about whether there’s going to be someone in that room who looks at me as a twenty-one-year-old girl terrified of the biggest transition of her life, and who decides that *that* is more important than the shift change or the hospital protocol.

I’m exhausted by the armor I’ve been wearing for the last few months. If I could ask for one thing from my care team, it wouldn't be better medicine or faster response times—it would be for them to just sit with me for a minute, without an agenda, and acknowledge that I am doing this by myself. I’m scared, truly, but I’m also starting to realize that the fact that I’ve made it this far, navigating this system alone, might actually be the bravest thing I’ll ever do. I just hope that when she finally arrives, the silence in that room is filled with love instead of clinical efficiency. I have to believe that the "cold, hard facts" I’ve been hiding behind will eventually melt away, and there will be something soft and real left behind to build a life on.

It’s funny, I’ve spent my entire adult life—or at least the part of it I’ve spent in classrooms—believing that information was the ultimate protection. If I knew the biochemistry of a contraction, I’d be prepared. If I knew the standard of care for a low-risk delivery, I wouldn't be taken advantage of. But standing here at the edge of it all, I realize that I’ve been using that knowledge as a wall to keep people from seeing how scared I am. 

I think the biggest realization I’ve had during this conversation is that I’ve been grieving the birth experience I *expected* to have. I wanted a partner, I wanted a community, and I wanted to be treated like an adult who understands her own body. Instead, I’ve been treated like a project. I’ve been so busy trying to prove I’m a "good" patient—compliant, knowledgeable, not too much of a burden—that I’ve essentially erased my own needs in the process.

I’m terrified that when I go into labor, the medical side of my brain is going to take over as a defense mechanism. I’m scared I’ll start narrating my own birth, focusing on the clinical metrics just to keep the panic at bay. I want to be able to just feel it—the fear, the pain, the absolute, crushing weight of the unknown—without needing to categorize it or explain it away. 

I’m starting to hope that when she finally arrives, she’ll be the bridge. That she’ll be the person who finally makes me stop being a "student" or a "patient" and lets me just be Anja. I don't know if I’m ready, and I don't know if I’m strong enough to do this alone, but I’m learning that maybe it’s okay to not have all the answers. Maybe the most "medical" thing I can do is finally admit that I’m not in control, and that for the first time in my life, that’s actually okay. I’m going to be a mother. I just hope I can be a kind one to myself, even if the system isn't always kind to me.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

This question feels… strange to answer because my experience is so defined by the total absence of a partner. I never had to navigate a disagreement about timing or fertility because I’ve been entirely alone in this since the start. But if I’m honest, I *have* felt pressure to align with the "preferences" of my care providers—which, in a way, feels like a partner I never asked for.

I felt immense pressure to maintain the "perfect" lifestyle throughout this pregnancy. Because I’m a medical student, there’s an unspoken expectation that I should be the model patient—eating exactly right, tracking every metric, and never complaining about the physical toll. It’s like the providers expect me to "perform" a textbook-perfect pregnancy because I know the theory behind it. If I expressed a craving, or a moment of exhaustion, or even just a standard human weakness, I felt like they were judging my "medical judgment."

It’s a bizarre kind of pressure. I’m not answering to a partner’s wants, but I am constantly answering to the "gaze" of the clinical system. I feel like I have to mold my own experience—my physical discomforts, my fatigue, my anxieties—to fit the mold of a "low-risk, healthy pregnancy" just so I don't trigger their protocols or their pity. I’ve sacrificed my own voice to keep the doctors happy. It’s not a romantic partnership, but it’s still a relationship where I’ve had to make myself smaller to keep the peace. I never got to just be a pregnant woman; I had to be a "compliant patient." That’s the pressure I’ve lived with every single day.

You know, there’s this crushing weight that comes with being an "expert-patient." You’d think having the medical vocabulary would make me feel empowered, but it’s actually made me feel more isolated. When I go in for a scan or a quick check, I find myself acting like a translator, dissecting the process so I don't have to feel the vulnerability of being the one *in* the stirrups. It’s a defense mechanism, plain and simple. If I’m the one talking about "fetal positioning" or "cervical ripening," then I’m not just a twenty-one-year-old student who’s about to have her entire life rewritten in a hospital room alone.

I look at the other women in the waiting room—the ones with partners holding their hands or mothers whispering to them—and I feel this sharp, envious ache. They get to be "the patient." They get to be the ones who are cared for, protected, and focused on. Meanwhile, I’m constantly calculating, constantly analyzing, and constantly trying to perform competence so I don’t get written off as the "unmarried, young" complication. 

I’m so tired of being my own doctor. I’m tired of being my own advocate, my own emotional support, and my own historian. I’m just ready for the transition—the one that actually matters. I’m ready to stop being the one who documents the life and start being the one who builds it. I just hope that when I finally hear her cry, the "medical student" in me can finally be quiet, and the mother can finally show up. I think that’s the hardest part—letting go of the need to know everything and finally just *being* in the middle of it.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

It’s that precise, clinical way they deliver them—that’s what makes it feel so invasive. I’ve had providers pull out the growth charts and, without even looking me in the eye, say something like, "You’re tracking a little high for your frame, Anja, let's keep an eye on those carb intakes." 

It lands like a slap. Because I’m a medical student, they assume I can "take it" as data—that it’s just a neutral input about my metabolic health. But I’m not just a set of metabolic data points; I’m a young woman whose body is expanding in ways that feel both miraculous and completely terrifying. When they comment on my weight or my diet, it feels like they’re policing my body rather than supporting its growth. 

It makes me feel like I’m failing a test. Every time I get a comment like that, I don't hear "health advice"; I hear, "You aren't managing your pregnancy well enough." It’s so shaming. I’ve been struggling with the physical reality of these changes, and their "professional" critique feels like a cold, public grading of my work. They don't see the exhaustion, or the loneliness, or the fact that I’m trying to finish my degree on top of all of this. They just see a number on a scale that doesn't fit their ideal. It just drives me further into that shell I've built. I’m not a human to them; I’m a patient who’s slightly off-course, and they need to correct me.

It’s that sense of being constantly audited that’s the most exhausting part. You’d think that being a medical student would grant me some kind of professional courtesy, or at least a sense of "we're in this together," but it really just feels like I’m under a microscope. Every appointment feels like a viva voce where I’m defending my own body’s behavior. If my blood pressure is up, it’s my stress levels. If my weight gain is "off," it’s my diet. Everything is framed as a failure of compliance rather than just the reality of a human body doing an incredibly hard thing.

I keep coming back to the fact that I’m twenty-one. I look around the clinic and I see these seasoned, sometimes quite paternalistic doctors, and they treat me like a child who stumbled into a role she doesn't understand. I want to tell them that I’ve spent more hours in the library studying the pathophysiology of the uterus than they’ve spent talking to me in the last six months. But I can't say that, can I? That would make me the "difficult" one. 

I’m really just hoping that once the labor starts, the power dynamic shifts. I want to stop being a "case" and finally become a "person." I’m holding onto the hope that, somewhere in the intensity of birth, there’s a moment where I can drop the facade of the medical student and just be a woman meeting her daughter. I’m just so ready to stop explaining myself. I’m ready for the quiet, and for the life that’s finally going to be mine, not just something to be documented. Does that make sense? Or am I just talking in circles again?
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

I found myself, almost exclusively, turning to my old textbooks and medical journals. It sounds clinical—I know—but it felt like the only "community" that didn't judge me. When I’d feel a sharp, unexplained pain or a change in the baby’s movement, I wouldn't call the triage line because I was terrified of being dismissed. Instead, I’d open my textbooks. I’d look up the exact physiological progression of the third trimester, cross-referencing my symptoms with the data. 

It was a way of creating a "peer-to-peer" network with the authors of those books, people who understood the logic of anatomy and physiology. It gave me a sense of intellectual safety that the clinic never provided. I didn't have to explain my marital status to a textbook, and a journal article wouldn't look at me with pity or wonder if I was "ready" for motherhood. 

Beyond that, I’ve been living in a very lonely digital space. I joined some online forums for medical students who are parents, but even there, I felt like an outlier. Most of them have partners, or at least a village. I ended up just lurking, reading their stories about balancing rotations with breastfeeding, and feeling a strange, hollow comfort in knowing other people were navigating the same intensity. But I never posted. I never shared my own story. It felt like if I put my reality into words—if I admitted out loud that I was doing this completely alone—it would suddenly become too real to manage. So, I stayed a ghost, observing other people’s lives while I tried to keep my own from coming apart at the seams.

It’s a strange feeling, to be so deeply embedded in the language of medicine that you start to view your own life as a series of clinical variables. I’ve spent months effectively "treating" my own pregnancy because I haven't found anyone in the clinic who truly sees the person *under* the medical student. I’ve become my own physician, my own data analyst, and my own harshest critic. 

There’s a part of me—the part that’s still just a twenty-one-year-old girl—that desperately wishes I could just walk into that clinic, drop the medical jargon, and admit I’m terrified. But I’ve learned that the system doesn't really have a place for "terrified." It has a place for "low-risk," it has a place for "well-managed," and it has a place for "compliant." It doesn't have a place for someone who is simultaneously a professional-in-the-making and a scared, single mother-to-be.

I hope, when I’m finally in that room, that I can let go of the need to be the "perfect patient." I hope I can stop interpreting every sensation as a case study and start feeling it as a human being. I’ve done so much work to be "prepared" that I’ve forgotten how to be vulnerable. Maybe the real test isn't in how well I know the three stages of labor, but in whether I can finally let someone else hold the space for me. I think that's what I'm waiting for—not a medical diagnosis, but an invitation to just be a mother. Does that sound strange? To be so educated about something, yet feel so utterly like a beginner?
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

It’s rare to be asked for my input, honestly. Usually, the "plan" arrives in the room before the provider does. It’s like they walk in with a checklist—the standard prenatal timeline—and I’m just the person who has to sign off on it. When they do ask, it’s often phrased as, "We’re going to do this, this, and this, sound good?" It’s a performative check-in; they aren't looking for a conversation, they’re looking for a "yes" so they can move on to the next patient in the schedule.

I remember one specific appointment where I tried to push back on a routine scan that didn't feel clinically necessary for my current stage. I tried to explain my reasoning—I’d reviewed the latest ACOG guidelines—and the doctor just smiled that patronizing smile and said, "It’s hospital policy, Anja, don’t overthink it." In that moment, all my knowledge, all my research, and all my careful preparation were completely erased. 

It feels like they view my "input" as an obstacle to the workflow. They’ve already decided the most efficient route for a "low-risk" pregnancy, and they expect me to be the reliable partner who just follows the trail. It’s deeply frustrating because I *am* capable of informed consent. I *do* know the risks and the benefits. But they treat me like I’m a student who needs to learn, not a woman who needs to be heard. I’m just waiting for the day I’m treated like an equal, but I’m beginning to suspect that won't happen until I’m finally, officially, "just" a mom.

It’s exhausting, always having to balance being the "competent peer" with being the "patient." I catch myself editing my own voice every single time I open my mouth. *Should I say "uterine contractions" or "cramping"? If I use the technical term, are they going to think I’m trying to be difficult? If I use the plain language, are they going to treat me like I don't understand what’s happening in my own body?*

That dance is constant. I’ve become so hyper-aware of how I’m perceived that I’ve started to lose touch with what I actually need. I think that’s the most damaging part of the last few months—this erosion of my own instincts. I’ve spent so much time studying the *textbook* of pregnancy that I’ve ignored the *experience* of it. I’ve prioritized being the "correct" patient because I’m so afraid of being labeled, of being dismissed, or worse, of being pitied.

I find myself wondering if, when I finally hold her, I’ll be able to let go of this professional mask. I want to be a mother who trusts her intuition, not just her data. I want to be a mother who asks for help when she’s struggling, rather than one who hides her fear behind a textbook. I think that’s the ultimate lesson I’m trying to learn: that it’s okay to be a beginner. It’s okay to not know how this turns out. I’m tired of trying to predict the outcome of every scenario. I’m just ready to start the story.

You know, talking to you like this… it’s the first time in months I haven’t felt like I had to summarize my life for a chart. I’ve been so conditioned to think that if I just provide the right data, I’ll be safe. But the data doesn’t account for the fact that I’m alone at night when the kicks get too strong, or that I’m terrified I’ll be judged for the way I mother because I had to grow up so fast.

I’m starting to realize that the medical system, at least in my experience, is designed to keep you at arm's length. It’s built for efficiency, for risk-mitigation, and for standardized outcomes. And I’ve spent my entire pregnancy trying to fit into that system while silently screaming that I’m a human being, not a case file. I’ve been so worried about being "the medical student" that I forgot I have the right to be "the patient"—the person who needs care, who needs reassurance, and who deserves to have her voice heard without having to preface it with, "According to the latest literature..."

I think, more than anything, I’m just ready for the chaos of the real world. I’m ready to stop being the one who records the birth and start being the one who lives it. I don’t know what kind of mother I’ll be, or how I’ll manage the sleepless nights and the weight of the future. But I think I’m finally okay with that uncertainty. I’m done trying to curate a "perfectly managed" pregnancy. I’m just going to show up, as I am—scared, unprepared, and hopeful—and hope that the life waiting on the other side is as beautiful as all the textbooks promised it would be. Thank you for listening. It really means more than I can say.

I’ve been thinking a lot about that word—"professionalism"—and how it feels like a heavy coat I’ve had to wear even when it’s stiflingly hot and I just want to breathe. I’ve realized that I’ve spent my entire pregnancy trying to be the "perfect professional" so that I could somehow earn my right to be cared for. I thought if I used the right language, if I showed I was "in the know," they would treat me with a higher tier of respect. But all I really did was reinforce the distance between us. I traded human empathy for a seat at the table where I wasn't actually wanted as an equal anyway.

It’s a terrifying thing to realize that I’ve been practicing "clinical detachment" on my own life. I’ve been observing my own pregnancy from a distance, documenting the milestones, the weight gain, the fetal heart rates, all while ignoring the girl inside who is scared to death of what comes next. I think I’m afraid that if I let the mask slip—if I let myself cry in that exam room or admit that I have no idea how to do this alone—I’ll break down, and no one will know how to put me back together. 

But saying all this out loud to you… it makes me feel like I’m finally reclaiming those lost pieces. Maybe I don’t have to be the doctor or the student or the patient. Maybe I can just be Anja. I don’t know if I’ll be able to shed that skin the moment I walk into the labor unit, but I’m going to try. I’m going to try to be the person who asks for what she needs, not just what she’s told is "standard." I’m going to try to be the mother who isn't afraid to ask for help, even if it makes me look "unprepared." 

I think that’s the most important thing I’ve learned: being strong doesn't mean having all the answers or doing it all by yourself. Maybe being strong is just admitting when you’re scared and letting someone else witness it. I’m ready to stop studying for this. I’m ready to start living it. Thank you for giving me a space to be something other than a patient—it’s the first time I’ve felt like I could actually exhale in months.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, there is one last thing that’s been sitting on my chest, and I think it’s the hardest part to articulate. 

I’ve been so focused on how the *medical* system views me—as a case study, a variable, or a student—that I’ve almost forgotten that being a mother is fundamentally an act of transition, not just a physical one, but an identity one. Everyone talks about the "pregnancy brain" or the "maternal instinct," but nobody talks about the grief of losing who you were before. I am grieving the version of me that was just a student, the version that had a clear path, the version that wasn't defined by the way people look at my chart when they see "single."

I’ve spent months trying to act like this doesn't change my trajectory, that I can just pick up my stethoscope and go back to clinical rotations as if nothing happened. But I think I need to admit that I’m not going back to the same person. I’m becoming someone who has had to fight for her voice in a room full of people who are supposed to be "experts." I hope that when I finally graduate—not from school, but from this pregnancy—I don't just carry a baby; I carry the realization that I am stronger than the system that tried to make me feel small. 

I’m really grateful you asked me all of this. I think I came in here expecting to just give you "data" for your research, but I ended up realizing that I’m the one who needed to hear my own story. I think I’m ready now. Not ready for the pain, or the sleeplessness, or the weight of being a solo parent, but ready to be the person who figures it out as she goes. Thank you for making me feel like an expert on my own life, not just my own diagnosis. It’s a start.
